title: Capstone: Fix a Broken Context Pipeline
concept: Diagnosing and repairing a context assembly with rot, poor ordering, and no compression; 'Next up: Advanced Context Engineering.'.

exercises.prompt: Quick recap: Which of these was NOT one of the common context engineering mistakes we covered last lesson?

options.text: Dumping entire documents into the context instead of relevant excerpts
Ignoring token budgets and overflowing the context window
Using a smaller AI model to save on computing costs

exercises.explain: Choosing a smaller model relates to cost/performance tradeoffs, not context engineering mistakes. The real mistakes we covered were dumping whole documents (instead of relevant pieces), ignoring token budgets, and burying the user's actual question in a wall of text.

cards.title: Your Bot Just Gave a Weird Answer
cards.body: You ask your AI assistant a simple question, but it replies with something from a conversation you had last week - totally unrelated. Something's broken in how its context was built. Time to play doctor and fix the pipeline!

cards.title: Fixing a Broken Pipeline
cards.body: Repairing a context pipeline means finding and fixing three common problems together: rot (stale, irrelevant, or outdated info), poor ordering (important stuff buried or scattered), and no compression (raw dumps instead of summaries). You diagnose first, then repair each piece.

cards.title: Think Like a Mechanic
cards.body: A broken context pipeline is like a messy toolbox: rusty tools (stale info) mixed with fresh ones, no order, and way too much stuff crammed in. A good mechanic sorts the toolbox first, keeps only what's needed for the job, and puts the most-used tools on top.

cards.title: Before and After
cards.body: BEFORE: A support bot's context includes a customer's entire 2-year ticket history, unsorted, with the current question tucked in the middle. AFTER: It includes a 3-sentence summary of past issues, the 2 most relevant recent tickets, and the current question clearly labeled at the end, right before the model responds.

cards.title: Myth: One Fix Solves Everything
cards.body: Some think removing old info alone fixes a broken pipeline. Not true! Even fresh, relevant info can fail if it's poorly ordered or never compressed. A real fix addresses rot, order, AND compression together - fixing just one leaves the others still broken.

cards.title: Broken vs. Repaired Pipeline
cards.body: Compare how the same information looks before and after a proper context engineering fix.

left.text: 5000 tokens of raw, unsorted history; stale facts mixed with new ones; question buried in the middle

right.text: 300-token summary of relevant history; only recent/relevant facts kept; question clearly placed for max attention

exercises.prompt: A pipeline has good ordering and no rot, but still overflows the token budget with raw, uncompressed text. What's still broken?

options.text: Nothing - ordering and freshness are all that matter
Compression is missing, so the pipeline may get truncated or cost too much
The pipeline needs more rot removed
The user's question should be deleted

exercises.explain: Even with fresh, well-ordered info, if it's never compressed (summarized/condensed), it can still overflow the token budget, get cut off, or cost more than necessary. Compression is a separate, essential repair step.

exercises.prompt: True or False: If you fix the ordering of a context pipeline so the question is placed well, you don't need to worry about removing outdated or irrelevant information.
exercises.explain: False! Ordering and rot are separate problems. Even perfectly placed context can mislead the model if it's stale or irrelevant. A full repair addresses rot, ordering, and compression all together, not just one piece.

exercises.sentence: When repairing a context pipeline, you should remove rot, apply compression, and fix the ordering so the most important content is easy for the model to find.
exercises.explain: Compression means summarizing or condensing information so it fits the token budget without losing the important meaning - a key repair step alongside removing rot and fixing order.

exercises.prompt: Which context pipeline description sounds like it was generated by an AI trying to sound impressive but is actually poorly engineered (rot + no compression + bad order)?

options.text: "Our pipeline filters context by recency and relevance, summarizes older content, and places the user's query prominently for the model to attend to."
"Leveraging comprehensive full-history integration, our system ingests all available data in raw form to ensure nothing is ever missed, appending the query wherever convenient."
"We compress long documents into short summaries and reorder sections so the most relevant parts appear closest to the question."

exercises.explain: Option B uses impressive-sounding words like 'comprehensive' and 'leveraging' but actually describes a broken pipeline: it dumps ALL raw data (no compression, likely includes rot) and places the query 'wherever convenient' (no intentional ordering). Good engineering, like options A and C, is specific about filtering, summarizing, and deliberate placement.

exercises.prompt: Final check: Your AI assistant's context pipeline pulls 5000 tokens of raw chat logs from 3 months ago, pastes them in random order, and puts the user's actual question at the very top of a huge wall of text (but the model still ignores it). What's the BEST fix, in order?

options.text: Delete the logs entirely so the pipeline has nothing to send
Filter out old/irrelevant logs (remove rot), summarize what's left (compress), then order by relevance with the user's question placed prominently near the end/top where the model attends most
Keep all 5000 tokens but ask the model nicely to focus on the question
Add even more historical data so the model has full context

exercises.explain: Fixing a broken pipeline means treating rot, ordering, and compression as separate but connected problems: (1) remove stale/irrelevant info so it doesn't mislead the model, (2) compress what remains (summarize) so you don't blow the token budget, and (3) place the most important content - especially the user's actual question - where the model is most likely to attend to it. Just deleting everything or piling on more data doesn't solve the real problem, and asking nicely doesn't fix a structural issue. This is the core workflow of context engineering: diagnose what's wrong, then repair each piece deliberately. Next up: Advanced Context Engineering.
